name: Wirecard
summary: German payment processor that fabricated €1.9B in nonexistent cash balances; collapsed into insolvency in 2020 after auditors and journalists exposed the fraud.
lesson: Audited financials and regulatory sign-off are not proof of legitimacy when a company controls its own auditors and regulators.

name: Katapult
summary: Rent-to-own e-commerce financing platform that went public via SPAC in 2021 at a ~$1.1B valuation; stock collapsed over 95% amid weak credit quality and was later delisted from Nasdaq.
lesson: SPAC valuations built on growth projections can mask fundamentally weak lending unit economics.

name: LendUp
summary: Online lender aimed at underbanked consumers that was repeatedly fined by the CFPB for deceptive practices and illegal fees, and shut down its lending operations in 2022.
lesson: Serving underbanked customers profitably requires compliance built in from day one, not bolted on after regulators intervene.

name: Frank
summary: Financial-aid application startup sold to JPMorgan Chase for $175M in 2021; JPMorgan later sued founder Charlie Javice alleging she fabricated millions of fake user accounts to inflate the sale price.
lesson: Acquirers must independently verify user metrics rather than relying on target-provided data during diligence.

name: Synapse Financial Technologies
summary: Banking-as-a-service middleware provider that collapsed into bankruptcy in 2024, leaving reconciliation discrepancies between partner banks and fintech apps that froze an estimated $85M+ in end-customer deposits.
lesson: In layered banking-as-a-service stacks, poor ledger reconciliation between middleware and partner banks can strand real customer money.

name: Bench Accounting
summary: Online bookkeeping and tax platform for small businesses that abruptly shut down in December 2024, locking out customers from their financial records before its assets were acquired by Employer.com.
lesson: Abrupt shutdowns of financial recordkeeping services can cause disproportionate harm to small-business customers relative to the company's own losses.

name: Plastiq
summary: Bill-payment platform that let businesses pay non-card-accepting vendors with a credit card; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in May 2023 after burning through venture funding without a durable margin.
lesson: Interchange-fee arbitrage business models are vulnerable when partner banks or card networks tighten terms.

name: Silvergate Bank
summary: California bank that pivoted to become the primary banking rail for crypto exchanges; collapsed in March 2023 after FTX's failure triggered a deposit run it could not cover without fire-selling securities.
lesson: Concentrating deposits in one volatile client sector creates correlated withdrawal risk that ordinary liquidity buffers cannot absorb.

name: Signature Bank
summary: New York bank with a large crypto and digital-asset deposit base that was closed by regulators in March 2023 amid contagion from Silicon Valley Bank's collapse and a rapid depositor run.
lesson: Uninsured deposit concentration can turn a solvent-on-paper bank into a forced closure within days once confidence breaks.

name: First Republic Bank
summary: Private bank serving high-net-worth clients that was seized by regulators and sold to JPMorgan in May 2023 after a wave of uninsured deposit withdrawals exposed large unrealized losses on low-rate mortgages.
lesson: Long-duration, low-yield assets funded by short-term deposits become a solvency risk once interest rates rise sharply.

name: Silicon Valley Bank
summary: Bank central to the startup ecosystem that failed in March 2023 after rising rates devalued its bond portfolio and a concentrated, tech-sector depositor base fled within 48 hours.
lesson: A depositor base concentrated in one interconnected industry can coordinate a bank run faster than traditional liquidity risk models assume.

name: FTX
summary: Crypto exchange that secretly funneled customer funds to its affiliated trading firm Alameda Research; collapsed into bankruptcy in November 2022 and its founder was later convicted of fraud.
lesson: Commingling customer custodial assets with a proprietary trading arm is a governance failure no amount of growth can offset.

name: Celsius Network
summary: Crypto lending and yield platform that promised high interest on deposits while making risky, undercollateralized bets; froze withdrawals and filed for bankruptcy in July 2022.
lesson: Yields well above market rates on 'safe' deposit products are usually funded by risk the platform isn't disclosing.

name: Voyager Digital
summary: Crypto brokerage offering interest-bearing accounts that filed for bankruptcy in July 2022 after its large uncollateralized loan to Three Arrows Capital went unpaid following the crypto market crash.
lesson: Extending large uncollateralized loans to counterparties concentrates risk that customer-facing yield products can't survive.

name: BlockFi
summary: Crypto lending platform that filed for bankruptcy in November 2022 after its exposure to FTX/Alameda, which had bailed it out earlier that year, became worthless in FTX's collapse.
lesson: A rescue financing deal from a counterparty with its own hidden problems can transmit rather than contain contagion.

name: Wonga
summary: UK payday lender once valued over £1B that collapsed into administration in August 2018, overwhelmed by a wave of customer compensation claims following a regulatory crackdown on payday lending.
lesson: A high-margin lending model built on regulatory gaps collapses once those gaps close and historical customers can claim redress retroactively.

name: Zenefits
summary: HR and health-insurance brokerage platform once valued at $4.5B that was found in 2016 to have used software letting unlicensed brokers sell insurance illegally; it was fined, restructured, and wound down as an independent company by 2022.
lesson: Growth-at-all-costs culture in a heavily regulated insurance distribution business invites existential compliance risk.

name: Fast (Fast.co)
summary: One-click checkout startup that shut down abruptly in April 2022 despite recent large fundraising, after reports surfaced of minimal revenue, poor merchant adoption, and unsustainable spending.
lesson: Impressive fundraising totals can mask an absence of real merchant demand for a product.

name: Kabbage
summary: SMB lending fintech whose core platform was acquired by American Express in 2020; the remaining legal entity (K Servicing) filed for bankruptcy in 2022 facing lawsuits over fraudulent PPP loans it had originated.
lesson: Loosening underwriting standards to move fast during a government relief program created liability that outlived the acquired business itself.

name: Terraform Labs (Terra/Luna)
summary: Creator of the algorithmic stablecoin UST and sister token LUNA, which de-pegged and collapsed in May 2022, wiping out roughly $40B in value; the company filed for bankruptcy in 2024 and its founder was later found liable for fraud.
lesson: Algorithmic stablecoins backed by a reflexive token rather than real reserves are structurally vulnerable to bank-run dynamics.

name: Xinja
summary: Australian neobank that launched with a licensed savings account offering a high interest rate; surrendered its banking license and returned deposits in December 2020 after failing to raise enough capital to sustain operations.
lesson: A neobank funding growth through an unsustainably high deposit rate needs a capital raise lined up well before the cash runs out.

name: Volt Bank
summary: Australia's first licensed neobank, which returned customer deposits and handed back its banking license in mid-2022 after a planned $100M capital raise fell through.
lesson: Regulatory capital requirements mean a neobank's survival can hinge entirely on one funding round closing on time.

name: Bó
summary: Digital-only bank launched by RBS (NatWest) in late 2019 to compete with Monzo and Starling; shut down just six months later in mid-2020 after failing to gain traction and to justify its cost amid COVID-19.
lesson: An incumbent bank spinning up a challenger brand still needs genuine differentiation, not just a new app skin.

name: Azlo
summary: No-fee digital banking app for freelancers and small businesses, backed by BBVA, that shut down in 2021 after BBVA sold its US banking operations to PNC and discontinued the unprofitable unit.
lesson: A fintech dependent on a single incumbent-bank sponsor is exposed to that sponsor's own strategic pivots.

name: Simple
summary: One of the earliest US neobanks, acquired by BBVA in 2014 for $117M; shut down in 2021 after PNC acquired BBVA USA and decided to consolidate customers onto its own digital platform.
lesson: Being an acquisition pioneer doesn't guarantee survival once the acquirer itself gets acquired.

name: Root Insurance
summary: Usage-based car insurance startup that IPO'd in 2020 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation; its stock has since fallen more than 95% amid persistent underwriting losses and high customer acquisition costs.
lesson: Telematics-based pricing didn't solve insurtech's fundamental problem: acquiring and retaining customers profitably in a thin-margin, heavily regulated line of business.

name: Metromile
summary: Pay-per-mile auto insurance startup that went public via SPAC in 2021 and was acquired by Lemonade in an all-stock deal in 2022 at a steep discount to its earlier valuation, after failing to reach profitable scale.
lesson: A distressed-sale acquisition can be the realistic best outcome once a capital-intensive insurtech's growth-at-a-loss model runs out of runway.

name: Clover Health
summary: Medicare Advantage insurtech taken public via a Chamath Palihapitiya SPAC in 2021; its stock collapsed over 90% amid short-seller allegations, an SEC inquiry, and persistent medical-cost losses.
lesson: SPAC hype cycles can inflate a health-insurance startup's valuation well beyond what its loss ratios can support.

name: Petal
summary: Credit-card fintech using cash-flow underwriting to serve credit-invisible consumers; sold its card portfolio to Prosper in 2023 and wound down its core consumer card-issuing business after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Alternative-underwriting credit products still require balance-sheet scale and cheap funding that early-stage fintechs struggle to secure.

name: Prosper Marketplace
summary: One of the original P2P lending marketplaces that underwent a major crisis in 2016 with mass layoffs and investor pullback, and has operated at sharply reduced scale in the years since.
lesson: Marketplace lenders depend on institutional investor confidence in loan quality, which can evaporate industry-wide after a single peer's scandal.

name: GreenSky
summary: Point-of-sale home-improvement lender acquired by Goldman Sachs in 2021 for $2.24B as part of its consumer banking push; Goldman sold it to Sixth Street in 2023 for roughly a quarter of that price as it retreated from consumer lending.
lesson: Even a well-established fintech can become a write-down when its acquirer's own strategic priorities change.

name: Stripe
summary: Payments infrastructure company that became the default API for online payments for millions of businesses; remains private with a valuation around $65-70B as of recent tender offers.
lesson: Deep developer-first infrastructure can win a market by making integration dramatically easier than incumbents, then expand into adjacent products.

name: Square (Block)
summary: Payments and point-of-sale company for small merchants that expanded into peer-to-peer payments (Cash App), banking, and crypto; publicly traded as Block Inc.
lesson: Starting with an underserved segment (small merchants excluded from traditional card processing) can build a base for a much larger financial ecosystem.

name: Adyen
summary: Dutch payments platform serving large global enterprises like Uber, Spotify, and Netflix through a single unified processing stack; one of Europe's most profitable fintech IPOs.
lesson: Focusing on large enterprise merchants with complex multi-country needs can command higher margins than mass-market SMB payments.

name: PayPal
funding: IPO 2002; spun off from eBay in 2015
summary: Pioneer of online consumer payments that became the dominant checkout method on eBay and across the web, and was spun off from eBay in 2015 as an independent public company.
lesson: Being the trusted default payment rail for an entire e-commerce ecosystem creates durable network effects that outlast the original host platform.

name: Klarna
summary: Swedish buy-now-pay-later and shopping platform that became one of Europe's most valuable fintechs; its valuation fell over 85% in 2022 during the funding downturn but recovered, and it filed to go public in the US in 2025.
lesson: Surviving a severe valuation reset requires real revenue and unit economics underneath the growth story, not just capital availability.

name: Affirm
summary: Buy-now-pay-later lender founded by Max Levchin that emphasizes transparent, interest-disclosed installment loans and has built deep retail partnerships including Amazon and Shopify.
lesson: Differentiating a BNPL product on transparent pricing (no late fees, clear APR) built merchant and consumer trust that pure convenience-focused rivals lacked.

name: Marqeta
summary: Card-issuing and payments infrastructure platform that powers Cash App, Square, DoorDash, and other fintechs' card programs via a modern, API-first issuer-processing stack.
lesson: Becoming the invisible infrastructure layer behind many other fintechs' products captures value across the whole industry's growth.

name: Plaid
summary: Financial data connectivity API that links bank accounts to fintech apps; a proposed $5.3B acquisition by Visa was blocked by the DOJ on antitrust grounds in 2021, and Plaid has continued operating independently.
lesson: Being deemed too competitively important to acquire can be validation of a defensible market position, not just a setback.

name: Chime
summary: The largest US neobank by account holders, offering fee-free checking and early paycheck access to primarily low- and middle-income customers; filed for a public offering in 2025.
lesson: Monetizing through interchange on everyday debit spending, rather than fees, aligned Chime's incentives with underserved customers' interests.

name: Revolut
summary: UK-founded 'super app' offering banking, currency exchange, crypto trading, and investing across dozens of countries; became Europe's most valuable fintech.
lesson: Bundling many financial services into one app increases engagement and monetization per user beyond a single-product neobank.

name: Wise (TransferWise)
summary: Cross-border money transfer service that undercut bank FX margins with transparent, mid-market-rate pricing; went public via a direct listing in London in 2021.
lesson: Radical fee transparency in a market known for hidden FX markups became a durable competitive advantage rather than just a marketing angle.

name: Nubank
summary: Brazilian neobank that became Latin America's most valuable fintech and one of the world's largest digital banks by customer count, offering no-fee credit cards and accounts to a historically underbanked population.
lesson: Emerging markets with large underbanked populations and expensive incumbent banks offer outsized product-market fit for a low-cost digital bank.

name: SoFi
summary: Started as a student-loan refinancing platform and expanded into a full digital bank after acquiring Golden Pacific Bancorp for a national bank charter in 2022, broadening into lending, investing, and banking products.
lesson: Acquiring a bank charter let SoFi fund its own loans at lower cost and diversify beyond its original student-lending niche.

name: Brex
summary: Corporate card and spend-management platform that started by serving venture-backed startups and later expanded into enterprise finance automation for larger companies.
lesson: A willingness to re-segment upmarket, from startups to larger enterprises, kept Brex's growth going once the smaller-company market matured.

name: Ramp
summary: Corporate card and expense-management platform that grew rapidly by emphasizing cost savings and automation for finance teams, becoming one of the fastest-growing SaaS-fintech companies in the US.
lesson: Positioning a corporate card product around saving customers money (not just convenience) created a sales pitch with a clear, measurable ROI.

name: Mercury
summary: Digital banking platform built for startups and SMBs, offering banking, treasury, and credit products through partner banks; grew to profitability while remaining relatively capital-efficient compared to peers.
lesson: Focusing narrowly on startup and SMB banking needs built strong loyalty in a specific niche.

name: Coinbase
summary: The largest US crypto exchange, which went public via direct listing in 2021 and survived the subsequent crypto winter and regulatory battles with the SEC to remain the dominant regulated US on-ramp for crypto.
lesson: Investing early in regulatory compliance and licensing gave Coinbase durability that many less-compliant crypto competitors lacked.

name: Circle
summary: Issuer of the USDC stablecoin that built deep partnerships across crypto exchanges and payment networks; went public in 2025 after an earlier SPAC attempt fell through in 2022.
lesson: Persisting through a failed SPAC merger and volatile crypto markets to eventually IPO shows the value of a genuinely reserve-backed, compliance-forward stablecoin model.

name: Remitly
summary: Digital remittance service focused on immigrant communities sending money to Latin America, Asia, and Africa, which scaled to serve millions of customers and went public in 2021.
lesson: Focusing product and marketing on specific immigrant corridors built trust and word-of-mouth growth that generic remittance apps couldn't match.

name: Wealthfront
summary: One of the original robo-advisors, offering automated portfolio management and tax-loss harvesting; a planned acquisition by UBS fell through in 2022, after which Wealthfront raised more capital and continued growing independently.
lesson: A failed acquisition doesn't have to be fatal if the underlying business has proven, durable product-market fit.

name: Betterment
summary: Pioneer robo-advisor that has continued to grow assets under management and expanded into cash management, retirement, and advisor-focused products, remaining one of the largest independent robo-advisors.
lesson: Automated low-fee investing found durable demand among younger investors underserved by traditional human advisors.

name: Lemonade
summary: AI-driven renters and homeowners insurance company using a behavioral-economics-inspired flat-fee model and a reinsurance-heavy balance sheet; grew premiums steadily post-IPO while still working toward full profitability.
lesson: An unconventional business model can double as an effective trust and marketing mechanism in insurance.

name: Next Insurance
summary: Digital small-business insurance provider offering fast online quotes for liability, workers' comp, and other SMB coverage; Munich Re acquired a majority stake in 2025 at a valuation over $2.6B.
lesson: Simplifying and speeding up SMB insurance underwriting created enough value that a major reinsurer chose to acquire rather than compete.

name: Starling Bank
summary: UK digital bank founded by Anne Boden that became one of the first UK neobanks to reach sustained profitability, driven by lending products and Banking-as-a-Service revenue alongside its retail app.
lesson: Diversifying revenue beyond interchange, into lending and B2B banking infrastructure, was key to reaching profitability where many neobank peers struggled.

name: Zopa
summary: World's first peer-to-peer lending platform, which wound down its original P2P investment product in 2021 and pivoted fully into becoming a licensed digital bank (Zopa Bank), reaching profitability in 2023.
lesson: Recognizing when a founding business model has run its course and pivoting decisively to a bank charter can extend a fintech's life.

name: LendingClub
summary: Marketplace lending pioneer that survived a major 2016 governance scandal and later acquired Radius Bank in 2021 to become a full digital bank, returning to sustained profitability.
lesson: Acquiring a bank charter gave LendingClub a cheaper, more stable funding source than relying entirely on institutional loan buyers.

name: Melio
summary: B2B payments platform helping small businesses pay and get paid via bank transfer or card, integrated with QuickBooks and other accounting software; reached unicorn status on strong SMB adoption.
lesson: Embedding a payments product directly inside SMBs' existing accounting workflow reduced friction and accelerated adoption.

name: Airwallex
summary: Australian-founded cross-border payments and banking infrastructure platform serving businesses globally; reached unicorn status with strong growth in Asia-Pacific, North America, and Europe.
lesson: Building a global multi-currency payments network from an Asia-Pacific base gave Airwallex an edge in cross-border corridors US-centric competitors underserved.

name: Tink
summary: European open banking platform providing account aggregation and payment-initiation APIs to banks and fintechs; acquired by Visa in 2022 for €1.8B, a strong exit for a European infrastructure fintech.
lesson: Building deep open-banking connectivity across fragmented European markets made Tink a strategic acquisition target for global card networks entering open banking.
